name: The Shire
description: The Four Farthings, from the Brandywine to the Far Downs. A kindly land, more even-tempered than the Eriador around it — Tolkien writes the Shire's weather as almost unnaturally placid, protected by some grace the Shire-folk themselves never notice. Rain is softer, winters shorter, storms rarer than in the wider Eriador.

spring.label: Fair and mild, the hedgerows greening, a cuckoo somewhere over the hills.
Soft spring showers between long spells of sun — good gardening weather.
Morning mist in the bottoms, gone by second breakfast.
An unseasonably warm still day — doors and windows open across the Farthings.
Grey and mild, neither quite rain nor quite dry.
A gentle all-day rain, the sort that makes everything green.
A late frost at dawn, gone before elevenses — the early blossom just spared.

summer.label: Warm and golden, the air thick with the smell of hay and pipeweed.
A hot still day, the Bywater pond full of hobbit children by noon.
Sunshine and brief showers, the old gaffer grumbling on his bench.
Fair with a light westerly — washing on every line in Hobbiton.
An afternoon thunderstorm, brief and loud, then sunshine again by supper.
Mild overcast, a little muggy — the hobbits complain mildly.
A full day of steady rain — an indoor day for pipe, pint, and gossip.

autumn.label: Crisp and fair, apples bright on every bough, woodsmoke in the evening air.
Thick golden mist in the lanes until mid-morning, burning off to clear skies.
Steady cool rain — good for the last of the root crops, bad for the harvest.
Low grey cloud, a damp chill, fires lit early in every smial.
A cool bright day, the leaves at their most brilliant — good walking weather.
Showers and sunshine in quick succession, rainbows over the Westfarthing.
The first hard frost — puddles iced at dawn, the grass whitened.

winter.label: Cool and grey, mild for the season — the old folk marvel at the easy winter.
Steady cold rain — muddy lanes, smials full of the smell of drying boots.
Hard frost, still and bright — smoke rising straight from every chimney.
Cold mist lying in the hollows until noon, the world hushed.
A light fall of snow, charming for a day before it turns to slush.
Clear and cold, stars hard and bright, the Old Took would have called it fair.
Sleet and wet snow — grumbling on every doorstep.
A real winter storm — a rarity in the Shire, talked about for years.
